For the iid sample case, $g_{i}\left(x_{i} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}\right) \equiv g\left(x_{i} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}\right)$. Using here $g(\cdot \mid \underline{\theta})$ for both the basic sample-size one pdf and for the probability distribution function of the full sample of size $n$ is a minor abuse of notation. However, we think that the reader will understand the meaning of the formulas and that it is better to minimize notation to facilitate comprehension of concepts.

A general estimator of $J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$ for TIC can be derived from (7.12):

$$
J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=\mathrm{E}_{f}\left[\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \log \left(g\left(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right)\right]\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \log \left(g\left(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right)\right]^{\prime}\right] .
$$

For the case of a random sample,

$$
\begin{aligned}
J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) & =\mathrm{E}_{f}\left[\left[\sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{\partial}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \log \left(g\left(x_{i} \mid \underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right)\right]\left[\sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{\partial}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \log \left(g\left(x_{i} \mid \underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right)\right]^{\prime}\right] \\
& =\sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathrm{E}_{f}\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \log \left(g\left(x_{i} \mid \underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right)\right]\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \log \left(g\left(x_{i} \mid \underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right)\right]^{\prime}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, we are led to use

$$
\hat{J}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{K}\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \log \left(g\left(x_{i} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}\right)\right)\right]\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \log \left(g\left(x_{i} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}\right)\right)\right]^{\prime} .
$$

A general version of TIC can be defined based on (7.36) and (7.37) (see, e.g., Shibata 1989:222):

$$
\mathrm{TIC}=-2 \log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}))+2 \operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{J}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[\hat{I}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right] .
$$

One selects the model that produces the smallest TIC. Because -TIC $/ 2=\hat{T}$ is for each model an asymptotically unbiased estimator of $\mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}[I(f, g(\cdot \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})))]$ -constant, the underlying optimization criterion is that we select the model that on average (over the set of models) minimizes this expected K-L information loss. For large $n$ this expected criterion is almost the same as minimizing the criterion $I(f, g)$ - constant; thus using (7.38), we are essentially targeting selecting the K-L best model of the set of models, and this is regardless of whether or not $f$ is in the model set.

The estimator $\hat{J}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$ converges to $J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$, and $\hat{I}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$ converges to $I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$, so TIC is asymptotically unbiased (i.e., consistent) as a selection criterion for the minimum expected $\hat{\mathrm{K}}$-L model. In practice this estimator of the trace term is so variable (and is not unbiased), even for large $n$, that it seems better to just use the parsimonious "estimator" $\widehat{\operatorname{tr}}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]=K$ (cf. Shibata 1989) (we will consider the matter further in later sections). This seems especially appropriate if we have done a good job of specifying our set of models from which to select a best-fitting model.

### 7.3.2 Bootstrap Estimation of TIC

The primary value of the bootstrap method herein is to assess model selection uncertainty based on applying an analytical model selection criterion (e.g., AIC, $\mathrm{AIC}_{c}, \mathrm{QAIC}_{c}$, or TIC based on formulas (7.33), (7.34), and (7.35)). However, a second and quite different use of the bootstrap can be made: Use some bootstrap method to estimate directly the quantity $T=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\theta}}}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}))]$; the K-L best model is the one that maximizes $\hat{T}$. Variations on this theme involve more direct bootstrap estimation of the key quantity $\operatorname{tr}\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \Sigma\right]$ (or
equivalently, $\left.\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]\right)$. We will describe a method designed to minimize the impact of approximations made in deriving (7.35).

From (7.24) and (7.26) (wherein $\underline{\hat{\theta}}$ denotes $\underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x}))$ we derive

$$
T \approx \mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}))]-\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{tr}\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \Sigma\right]-\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{tr}\left[\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}[\hat{I}(\underline{\hat{\theta}})]\left[\underline{\theta}_{o}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]\left[\underline{\theta}_{o}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]^{\prime}\right] .
$$

Hence, a model selection criterion can be based on

$$
\hat{T}=\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}))-\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{I}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \hat{\Sigma}\right]-\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{\mathrm{E}_{x}}[\hat{I}(\underline{\hat{\theta}})]\left[\underline{\theta}_{o}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]\left[\underline{\theta}_{o}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]^{\prime}\right]
$$

Additional approximations applied to (7.39), or to the basic derivations, lead to

$$
\hat{T}=\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}))-\operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{I}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \hat{\Sigma}\right]
$$

which could also be the basis for a bootstrap estimator (as could (7.35)).
We assume that the sample structure allows a meaningful bootstrap sampling procedure (easily done in the iid sample case). Let a bootstrap sample be denoted by $\underline{x}^{*}$ with corresponding bootstrap MLE $\underline{\hat{\theta}}^{*}$. The needed likelihood second partial derivatives will have to be determined either analytically or numerically. To avoid more notation, we do not index the bootstrap samples, but rather just note that needed summations are over $B$ bootstrap samples.

In the bootstrap estimators, the MLE $\underline{\hat{\theta}}$ plays the role of $\underline{\theta}_{o}$. Hence bootstrap estimators of $I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right), \Sigma$, and $\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}[\hat{I}(\underline{\hat{\theta}})]\left[\underline{\theta}_{o}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]\left[\underline{\theta}_{o}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]^{\prime}$ are

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{I}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) & =-\frac{1}{B}\left[\sum_{B} \frac{\partial^{2} \log \left(g\left(\underline{x}^{*} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}\right)\right)}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}}\right], \\
\hat{\Sigma} & =\frac{1}{B}\left[\sum_{B}\left[\underline{\hat{\theta}}^{*}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]\left[\underline{\hat{\theta}}^{*}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]^{\prime}\right], \\
\hat{\mathrm{E}}_{\underline{x}}\left[[ \hat { I } ( \underline { \hat { \theta } } ) ] \left[\underline{\theta}_{o}\right.\right. & \left.-\underline{\hat{\theta}}]\left[\underline{\theta}_{o}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]^{\prime}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{B}\left[\sum_{B}\left[-\frac{\partial^{2} \log \left(g\left(\underline{x}^{*} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}^{*}\right)\right)}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}}\right]\left[\underline{\hat{\theta}}^{*}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]\left[\underline{\hat{\theta}}^{*}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]^{\prime}\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

These estimators mimic the expectation over $f$, because the sample arises from $f$, the bootstrap resamples the sample, and under any model our best estimator of $\underline{\theta}_{o}$ is the MLE $\underline{\hat{\theta}}$ (note that $\underline{\theta}_{o}$ varies by model $g$ ). One should use the same $B$ bootstrap samples with every model in the set of models over which selection is made.

The above suffices to compute TIC as

$$
\mathrm{TIC}=-2 \log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}))+2 \operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{I}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \hat{\Sigma}\right]
$$

using (7.40) and (7.41) (this is estimating the same quantity as TIC, so we call it TIC here, because $J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}=I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \Sigma$ ). To use (7.39) for bootstrapbased model selection, base the estimation of its second and third components on (7.40), (7.41), and (7.42); or in a form analogous to AIC and TIC, the model selection criterion to minimize is

$$
-2 \log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}))+\operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{I}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \hat{\Sigma}\right]+\operatorname{tr} \hat{\mathrm{E}}_{\underline{x}}\left[[\hat{I}(\hat{\hat{\theta}})]\left[\underline{\theta}_{o}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]\left[\underline{\theta}_{o}-\underline{\hat{\theta}}\right]^{\prime}\right] .
$$

It may well be that (7.43), i.e., TIC, would suffice and (7.44) is not a better estimator of $-2 T$.

Recent work on this use of the bootstrap to find $\hat{T}$ for K-L-based model selection is found in Ishiguro, et al. (1997), Cavanaugh and Shumway (1997), Shao (1996) and Chung et al. (1996). Shibata (1997a) has considered, in a general context, theoretical properties of many alternative implementations of the bootstrap to estimate the needed model selection criterion $T$. He notes that there is no unique way to do this bootstrapping to estimate the relative K-L model selection criterion, but that all reasonable bootstrap implementations are asymptotically equivalent to TIC. This use of the bootstrap has the advantage of bypassing concerns about all approximations used to get TIC or AIC. Despite this apparent advantage, Shibata (1997a, page 393) concludes that there is no reason to use the bootstrap this way to compute $\hat{T}$. It probably suffices to use a simple nonbootstrap computation of $\hat{T}$ (in particular, $\mathrm{AIC}_{c}$ ).

It should thus be clear that there are two very different ways to use the bootstrap in model selection. Not much used is the case of getting a single estimate of $T$ for each model based on the full set of bootstrap samples. The more common (and more useful) use of the bootstrap in model selection is first to accept some easily computable model selection criterion, such as AIC, and then to apply that criterion to all models considered for all the bootstrap samples created (and tabulate results like frequency of selection of each model). This use of the bootstrap leads to information about inference uncertainties after model selection. [There is also a large literature on use of the bootstrap under non-K-L-based model selection; see, e.g., Breiman 1992; Efron 1983, 1986; Hjorth 1994; Linhart and Zucchini 1986; and Shao 1996.]

## 7.4 $\mathrm{AIC}_{c}$ : A Second-Order Improvement

### 7.4.1 Derivation of $A I C_{c}$

The results above are completely general, and as such do not lead to some of the more specific results in the literature. In particular, if we assume a univariate linear structural model with homogeneous, normally distributed errors, conditional on any regressor variables, we can get the results of Hurvich and

Tsai $(1989,1995$ b) (see also Sugiura 1978). We let the model structure be

$$
\mu_{i}=\mathrm{E}\left(x_{i} \mid \underline{z}\right)=\sum_{j=1}^{K-1} z_{i j} \beta_{j}, \quad i=1, \ldots, n
$$

More specifically (but without explicitly denoting the conditioning on "regressors" $\underline{z}_{i}$ ),

$$
x_{i}=\sum_{j=1}^{K-1} z_{i j} \beta_{j}+\epsilon_{i}, \quad i=1, \ldots, n,
$$

where the $\epsilon_{i}$ are iid normal ( $0, \sigma^{2}$ ). There are thus $K$ parameters making up $\underline{\theta}$ ( $\sigma^{2}$ is the $K$ th one), and $g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta})$ is given by the multivariate-normal $\left(\underline{\mu}, \sigma^{2} I\right)$ distribution (MVN); $I$ is the $n \times n$ identity matrix. If we let $f \equiv g$ or $\bar{f} \subset g$, then we can derive the $\mathrm{AIC}_{c}$ results of Hurvich and Tsai. This last notation means that either $g$ is the true data-generating "model," or $f$ is actually the same distribution and structural form as model $g$ but with one or more elements of $\underline{\theta}$ set to 0 (hence there are superfluous parameters). The superfluous parameters serve only to increase $K$; hence the simplest way to get $\mathrm{AIC}_{c}$ is to assume this regression model $g$ and assume that $f \equiv g$. The derivation is given below in some detail because of the importance of AIC ${ }_{c}$.

Matrix notation is simpler to use, and hence $\underline{X}=Z \underline{\beta}+\underline{\epsilon}$ and $\mathrm{E}(\underline{X})=\underline{\mu}$. Without loss of generality we assume that $Z$ ( $n$ by $K-\overline{1}$ ) is of full rank. The likelihood is

$$
g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta})=\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}}\right]^{n}\left[\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\right]^{n / 2} \exp \left[-\frac{1}{2} \frac{(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})^{\prime}(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})}{\sigma^{2}}\right]
$$

and we are here taking $f \equiv g$. Ignoring additive constants and simplifying, the log-likelihood can be taken as

$$
\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta}))=-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\sigma^{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \frac{(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})^{\prime}(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})}{\sigma^{2}} .
$$

The MLEs are well known here:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\underline{\hat{\beta}} & =\left(Z^{\prime} Z\right)^{-1} Z^{\prime} \underline{X} \\
\hat{\sigma}^{2} & =\frac{(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})^{\prime}(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})}{n}
\end{aligned} .
$$

Therefore,

$$
\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))=-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \frac{(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})^{\prime}(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})}{\hat{\sigma}^{2}} ;
$$

hence, the maximized log-likelihood is

$$
\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))=-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)-\frac{n}{2}
$$

(the constant $-n / 2$ can be dropped in practice).
We want to determine the bias if we use $\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))$ as an estimator of our target

$$
T=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})))],
$$

where $\underline{x}$ and $\underline{y}$ are two independent random samples of size $n$. To make the evaluation here we actually use the specified form of the model (and of course take expectations with respect to $f \equiv g$ ). Hence, we want (a simplified notation is used here)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})))] & \\
& =\mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}_{y}^{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)^{\prime}\left(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)}{\hat{\sigma}_{y}^{2}}\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

The order of integration was reversed for the right-hand side above. Thus our first task is to evaluate

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left[\left(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta}_{y}\right)^{\prime}\left(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta}_{y}\right)\right] \\
&=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left[\left((\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})+\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\beta}_{y}\right)\right)^{\prime}\left((\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})+\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\beta}_{y}\right)\right)\right] \\
&=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left[(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})^{\prime}(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})\right]+\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left[2\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\beta}_{y}\right)^{\prime}(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})\right] \\
&+\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left[\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)^{\prime}\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)\right] \\
&=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left[(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})^{\prime}(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\beta})\right]+\left[2\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}(\underline{X})-Z \underline{\beta}\right)\right] \\
&+\left[\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)^{\prime}\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

The middle term above vanishes because $\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}(\underline{X})=Z \underline{\beta}$. Also, the first of the three terms above is identical to $\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left(\underline{\epsilon}^{\prime} \underline{\epsilon}\right)=n \sigma^{2}$. So we have the result

$$
\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left[\left(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)^{\prime}\left(\underline{X}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)\right]=n \sigma^{2}+\left[\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)^{\prime}\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)\right] .
$$

Using this partial result we have

$$
T=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}_{y}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})}\left[\frac{n \sigma^{2}+\left[\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)^{\prime}\left(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}}_{y}\right)\right]}{\hat{\sigma}_{y}^{2}}\right] .
$$

The first term above does not need to be evaluated further because it is also the leading term in the expected log-likelihood. Also, at this point, we can drop the designation of $\underline{\theta}$ as being based on sample $\underline{y}$. The designations $\underline{x}$ or $\underline{y}$ are really just dummy arguments in integrals. Consequently, in the above, the notation
could be in terms of $\underline{y}$ or $\underline{x}$, or this notation can just be dropped. Thus we have

$$
T=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\theta}}}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\theta}}}\left[\frac{n \sigma^{2}+\left[(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})^{\prime}(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})\right]}{\hat{\sigma}^{2}}\right]
$$

Now we make use of another well-known result in theoretical statistics: Under a linear model structure with errors as iid normal $\left(0, \sigma^{2}\right)$, the MLE's $\underline{\hat{\beta}}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^{2}$ are independent random variables. Therefore, the second expectation term in (7.45) partitions into two multiplicative parts, as follows:

$$
T=\mathrm{E}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\beta}}}\left[n \sigma^{2}+\left[(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})^{\prime}(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})\right]\right] \mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\sigma}}^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}^{2}}\right]
$$

As a next step, rewrite the needed expectation of the quadratic form in the above as

$$
\mathrm{E}\left[(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})^{\prime}(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})\right]=\operatorname{tr}\left[\left(Z^{\prime} Z\right) \mathrm{E}\left[(\underline{\hat{\beta}}-\underline{\beta})(\underline{\hat{\beta}}-\underline{\beta})^{\prime}\right]\right]
$$

The expectation on the right-hand side above, i.e., $\mathrm{E}\left[(\underline{\hat{\beta}}-\underline{\beta})(\underline{\hat{\beta}}-\underline{\beta})^{\prime}\right]$, is the sampling variance-covariance matrix of $\hat{\beta}$, which is known to be $\sigma^{2}\left(Z^{\prime} Z\right)^{-1}$. Thus, for the $K-1$ square identity matrix $I$,

$$
\mathrm{E}\left[(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})^{\prime}(Z \underline{\beta}-Z \underline{\hat{\beta}})\right]=\operatorname{tr}\left[\sigma^{2} I\right]=\sigma^{2}(K-1)
$$

Putting it all together to this point in the derivation, we have

$$
T=\mathrm{E}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{1}{2}\left[(n+K-1) \sigma^{2}\right] \mathrm{E}_{\hat{\sigma}^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}^{2}}\right]
$$

To finish the process we relate $\hat{\sigma}^{2}$ to a central chi-squared random variable, namely $\chi_{\mathrm{df}}^{2}$ on $n-(K-1)$ degrees of freedom, df . These results also are well known in statistical theory:

$$
\frac{n \hat{\sigma}^{2}}{\sigma^{2}} \sim \chi_{n-K+1}^{2}
$$

So we now rearrange (7.46) to be

$$
\begin{aligned}
T & =\mathrm{E}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{1}{2}[(n+K-1) n] \mathrm{E}\left[\frac{1}{n \hat{\sigma}^{2} / \sigma^{2}}\right] \\
T & =\mathrm{E}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{n}{2}(n+K-1) \mathrm{E}\left[\frac{1}{\chi_{n-K+1}^{2}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Yet another known exact result is

$$
\mathrm{E}\left[\frac{1}{\chi_{d f}^{2}}\right]=\frac{1}{\mathrm{df}-2}
$$

(assuming $\mathrm{df}>2$ ).



Using the last result above we have reduced (7.46) to

$$
T=\mathrm{E}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{n}{2}(n+K-1)\left[\frac{1}{n-K-1}\right] .
$$

This result is exact. No approximations were made in its derivation; however, it applies only to the particular context of its derivation, which includes the constraint $f \subseteq g$. Some more simplification of (7.47):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T & =\mathrm{E}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{n}{2}\left[\frac{n+K-1}{n-K-1}\right] \\
& =\mathrm{E}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{n}{2}\left[1+\frac{2 K}{n-K-1}\right] \\
& =\mathrm{E}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]-\frac{n}{2}-\frac{n K}{n-K-1} \\
& =\mathrm{E}\left[-\frac{n}{2} \log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)-\frac{n}{2}\right]-\frac{n K}{n-K-1} .
\end{aligned}
$$

The term above within the expectation operator is the maximized loglikelihood. Thus we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
T & =\mathrm{E}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))]-\frac{n K}{n-K-1} \\
& =\mathrm{E}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))]-\frac{(n-K-1+K+1) K}{n-K-1} \\
& =\mathrm{E}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))]-K-\frac{K(K+1)}{n-K-1} .
\end{aligned}
$$

If we convert this to an AIC result, we have, as an exact result in this context,

$$
\begin{aligned}
-2 T & =-2 \mathrm{E}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))]+2 K+\frac{2 K(K+1)}{n-K-1} \\
& =\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{AIC})+\frac{2 K(K+1)}{n-K-1}=\mathrm{E}\left(\mathrm{AIC}_{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This result thus motivates use of the term $2 K(K+1) /(n-K-1)$ as a small-sample-size bias-correction term added to AIC. The result assumes a fixed-effects linear model with normal errors and constant residual variances. Under different sorts of models, a different small-sample correction to AIC arises (the matter is explored some in the next subsection). However, the result given by (7.48) seems useful in other contexts, especially if $n$ is large but $K$ is also large relative to $n$. Without exception, if sample size $n$ is small, some sort of "AIC ${ }_{c}$ " is required for good model selection results, and we recommend (7.48) unless a more exact small-sample correction to AIC is known.

### 7.4.2 Lack of Uniqueness of $A I C_{c}$

The result in (7.48) is not universal in that other assumed univariate (and more so for multivariate) models, with $g=f$ to facilitate a derivation, or ways of deriving a small-sample adjustment to AIC, will lead to different adjustment terms. This section is just a brief elaboration of this idea.

The simplest case to present arises for a situation analogous to one-way ANOVA, but we let the within-subgroup variance differ for each subgroup. This can be generalized to having $m$ subsets of data, each of sample size $n_{i}$, and the full model is as used in Section 7.4.1 above, but with parameter set $\{\underline{\theta}\}=\left\{\underline{\beta}_{i}, \sigma_{i}^{2}, i=1, \ldots, m\right\}$ (this might be a global model in some cases). Let each parameter subset be of size $K_{i}$; hence $K=K_{1}+\cdots+K_{m}$. It should be almost obvious, after some thought, that for this situation the small-sample correction to AIC is

$$
-2 T=-2 \mathrm{E}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))]+2 \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[K_{i}+\frac{2 K_{i}\left(K_{i}+1\right)}{n_{i}-K_{i}-1}\right]
$$

hence,

$$
\mathrm{AIC}_{c}=-2 \log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))+2 K+\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[\frac{2 K_{i}\left(K_{i}+1\right)}{n_{i}-K_{i}-1}\right]
$$

The reason that there are $m$ "correction" terms is that we had to estimate $m$ different variance parameters. One can thus envision many other models where the form of AIC must differ from that of the simple normal-model case with only one estimated $\sigma^{2}$.

Another informative exact calculation of the bias term, $T-\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}))]$, is obtained for the case of the model and truth being the one-parameter negative exponential distribution (hence $K=1$ ):

$$
g(x \mid \lambda)=\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-x / \lambda}
$$

For an iid sample from $g(x \mid \lambda)$, let $S=x_{1}+\cdots+x_{n}$. Then

$$
\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \lambda))=-n \log (\lambda)-S / \lambda
$$

The MLE is $\hat{\lambda}=S / n$, so

$$
\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \hat{\lambda}))=-n \log (\hat{\lambda})-n .
$$

The target to be unbiasedly estimated is

$$
T=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[-n \log (\hat{\lambda})-S_{\underline{y}} / \hat{\lambda}\right]
$$

where the sum $S_{\underline{y}}$ is based on an independent sample of size $n$, while $\hat{\lambda}$ is based on sample $\underline{x}$. It is easy to evaluate the above $T$ to be

$$
T=\mathrm{E}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \hat{\lambda}))]+n-n^{2} \lambda \mathrm{E}\left[\frac{1}{S}\right] .
$$

The sum $S$ is a random variable with a simple gamma distribution; hence the expectation of $1 / S$ in (7.49) is known to be exactly $1 /(\lambda(n-1))$. It is thus easy to derive the exact result, expressed in "AIC" form (i.e., as $-2 T$ ):

$$
-2 T=-2 \mathrm{E}(\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \hat{\lambda})))+2+\frac{2}{n-1} .
$$

Recall that here $K=1$, so the corresponding total bias-correction term under the $\mathrm{AIC}_{c}$ form would be $2+4 /(n-2)$. The point is that the exact form of AIC would be 2 K plus a small-sample correction term that would vary according to the model assumed. It is reasonable to think that this small-sample correction term should be $O(1 / n)$.

Theoretically, when $f \subset g$ the error in using $K$ as the bias correction to $\hat{T}=\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}))$ is always $O(1 / n)$, and Hurvich and Tsai's form seems like a good general choice. There is, however, considerable need for research on improved bias terms for $\mathrm{AIC}_{c}$-type criteria. In this regard, an area offering research opportunities is that of when the random variable is discrete (see, e.g., Sugiura 1978, Shibata 1997b), such as Poisson, binomial, or Bernoulli (hence also logistic regression), because then we can get parameter MLEs taking on the value 0 . This creates a problem in evaluating the theoretical target model selection criterion because we encounter the need to compute $y \cdot \log (0)$, which is not defined (see, e.g., Burnham et al. 1994). AIC is still defined, but its small-sample properties are now more problematic, as is the small-sample bias-correction term needed to define an AIC ${ }_{c}$. Operating characteristics of AIC-based model selection for count-type data need more study for small sample sizes.

### 7.5 Derivation of AIC for the Exponential Family of Distributions

A generalization of normality-based models is found in the exponential family of distributions. The realizations that (1) many common applications of statistical analyses are based on exponential family models, and (2) normality-based regression is in the exponential family and leads to exact K-L model selection results (i.e., $\mathrm{AIC}_{c}$ ) motivated us to show the derivation of AIC theory under this restricted but very useful case. The canonical representation of an exponential family pdf involves sums of functions of the sample values. It is convenient to denote these sums by $S_{j}$.

A suitable canonical representation for the exponential family of probability distributions is

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta}) & =\exp \left[\left[\sum_{j=1}^{K} S_{j} \theta_{j}\right]+H(\underline{\theta})+G(\underline{S})\right] \\
& =\exp \left[\underline{S}^{\prime} \underline{\theta}+H(\underline{\theta})+G(\underline{S})\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

Each $S_{j}$ is a function (hence $S_{j}(\underline{x})$ ) of the full sample $\underline{x}=\underline{\mu}(\underline{\theta})+\underline{\epsilon}$ and any covariates $Z$ involved in representing $\underline{\mu}(\underline{\theta})$, on which we condition. The $K$-element vector of sufficient statistics is $\overline{\underline{S}}=\left(S_{1}, \ldots, S_{K}\right)^{\prime}$.

In the canonical representation of (7.50) the parameter $\underline{\theta}$ is generally some 1-to-1 transformation of another $K$-dimensional parameter of direct interest. There is no loss of generality in allowing any such 1-to-1 transformation. We will revisit this matter and show why it is so at the end of this section.

Our goal is to evaluate

$$
T=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\log \left(g\left(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right)\right)\right]=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\underline{S}_{x}^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}+H\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right)+G\left(\underline{S}_{x}\right)\right] .
$$

Here, $\underline{S}_{x}$ and $\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}$ are thought of as based on independent samples $\underline{x}$ and $\underline{y}$. We also simplified the notation, now using $\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}$ rather than $\underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})$.

Formula (7.51) above can be rewritten as

$$
\begin{aligned}
T & =\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\left(\underline{S}_{x}-\underline{S}_{y}+\underline{S}_{y}\right)^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}+H\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right)+G\left(\underline{S}_{x}\right)\right] \\
& =\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\left(\underline{S}_{x}-\underline{S}_{y}\right)^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}+\underline{S}_{y}^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}+H\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right)+G\left(\underline{S}_{x}\right)\right] \\
& =\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\underline{S}_{y}^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}+H\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right)+G\left(\underline{S}_{x}\right)\right]+\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\left(\underline{S}_{x}-\underline{S}_{y}\right)^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right] \\
& =\left[\mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left(\underline{S}_{y}^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}+H\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right)\right)+\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left(G\left(\underline{S}_{x}\right)\right)\right]+\mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\left(\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left(\underline{S}_{x}\right)-\underline{S}_{y}\right)^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

The interchangeability of integration arguments now is used. This is permissible because both expectations are with respect to $f$; hence $\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left(G\left(\underline{S}_{x}\right)\right)= \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left(G\left(\underline{S}_{y}\right)\right)$. Also, for simplicity we will use $\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}\left(\underline{S}_{x}\right)=\mathrm{E}(\underline{S})$, and now we get

$$
\left.T=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\underline{S}_{y}^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}+H\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right)\right)+G\left(\underline{S}_{y}\right)\right]+\mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\left(\mathrm{E}(\underline{S})-\underline{S}_{y}\right)^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right]
$$

Changing the argument from $y$ to $x$ in the first part above, just to emphasize the result for this exponential family case, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
T & =\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}(\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}})))+\mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\left(\mathrm{E}(\underline{S})-\underline{S}_{y}\right)^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right] \\
& \equiv \mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}(\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}})))-\mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\left(\underline{S}_{y}-\mathrm{E}(\underline{S})\right)^{\prime} \underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Formula (7.52) is an exact result and clearly shows the bias to be subtracted from $\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}(\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}})))$ to get $T$ :

$$
\operatorname{Bias}=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[\left(\underline{S}_{y}-\mathrm{E}(\underline{S})\right)^{\prime}\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}-\underline{\theta}_{*}\right)\right]
$$

The notation used here is $\mathrm{E}\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}_{y}\right)=\underline{\theta}_{*}$ to denote the exact expectation of the MLE for the given sample size $n$ and model $g ; \underline{\theta}_{o} \approx \underline{\theta}_{*}$ with asymptotic equality.

To further simplify notation, now that only one "sample" is involved, we use

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \operatorname{Bias}=\mathrm{E}\left[(\underline{S}-\mathrm{E}(\underline{S}))^{\prime}\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{*}\right)\right] \\
& \operatorname{Bias}=\operatorname{tr} \mathrm{E}\left[\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{*}\right)(\underline{S}-\mathrm{E}(\underline{S}))^{\prime}\right]=\operatorname{tr}[\operatorname{COV}(\underline{\hat{\theta}}, \underline{S})] .
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence for the exponential family an exact result is

$$
T=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}(\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}})))-\operatorname{tr}[\operatorname{COV}(\underline{\hat{\theta}}, \underline{S})]
$$

(something similar appears in Bonneu and Milhaud 1994). The $K \times K$ matrix of covariance elements, $\operatorname{COV}(\underline{\hat{\theta}}, \underline{S})$, can be approximated by Taylor series methods. If the exact covariance matrix can be found, then we have an exact result for the needed bias term above (Hurvich and Tsai 1989, in effect, did such an exact evaluation for the normal distribution case). The result (7.54) may seem not very useful because it seems to apply only to the canonical form of the exponential family. This is not true; the matter of generality of the canonical result will be addressed below.

Before further evaluation of the bias term, we consider the MLEs and the Hessian. First,

$$
\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta}))=\underline{S}^{\prime} \underline{\theta}+H(\underline{\theta})+G(\underline{S}),
$$

so

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta}))}{\partial \underline{\theta}} & =\underline{S}+\frac{\partial H(\underline{\theta})}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \\
\frac{\partial^{2} \log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta}))}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}} & =\frac{\partial^{2} H(\underline{\theta})}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

and thus

$$
I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=\mathrm{E}_{f}\left[-\frac{\partial^{2} \log \left(g\left(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right)}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}}\right]=-\frac{\partial^{2} H\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}} .
$$

It follows that the MLE satisfies

$$
\underline{S}=-\frac{\partial H(\underline{\hat{\theta}})}{\partial \underline{\theta}}
$$

It is worth noting here that $\underline{\theta}_{o}$ satisfies

$$
\mathrm{E}_{f}(\underline{S})=-\frac{\partial H\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)}{\partial \underline{\theta}}
$$

This is an exact result, whereas $\mathrm{E}(\underline{\hat{\theta}}) \approx \underline{\theta}_{o}$ is (in general) only $O(1 / \sqrt{n})$.
The formula for $J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$, based on (7.12), becomes

$$
J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=\mathrm{E}_{f}\left[\underline{S}-\mathrm{E}_{f}(\underline{S})\right]\left[\underline{S}-\mathrm{E}_{f}(\underline{S})\right]^{\prime}
$$

which is the true variance-covariance matrix of $\underline{S}$. The $n$ iid observations produce a set of statistics $\underline{S}_{i}$ that sum to $\underline{S}$; hence an estimator of $J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$ as given by (7.56) is

$$
\hat{J}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=\frac{n}{n-1}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\underline{s}_{i}-\underline{\bar{s}}\right]\left[\underline{s}_{i}-\underline{\bar{s}}\right]^{\prime}\right]
$$

Returning now to the evaluation of the bias term, a first-order Taylor series expansion gives us

$$
-\frac{\partial H(\underline{\hat{\theta}})}{\partial \underline{\theta}} \approx-\frac{\partial H\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)}{\partial \underline{\theta}}-\frac{\partial^{2} H\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}}\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) ;
$$

hence

$$
\underline{S} \approx \mathrm{E}(\underline{S})+I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right), \quad O_{p}(1 / \sqrt{n}) .
$$

Inserting (7.58) into the exact result (7.53) as well as also using $\underline{\theta}_{o}$ to approximate $\underline{\theta}_{*}$ (inasmuch as we are now replacing an exact result with an approximate result anyway), we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Bias} & \approx \mathrm{E}\left[\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{\prime}\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right] \\
& =\mathrm{E}\left[\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)^{\prime} I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right] \\
& =\mathrm{E} \operatorname{tr}\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)^{\prime}\right] \\
& =\operatorname{tr}\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \mathrm{E}\left[\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left(\underline{\hat{\theta}}-\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)^{\prime}\right]\right]=\operatorname{tr}\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \Sigma\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus we have shown that in this common case of an exponential family model,

$$
T \approx \mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}(\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}})))-\operatorname{tr}\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \Sigma\right]
$$

(the approximation is to $O(1 / n)$ ). Note that this derivation did not encounter any problems like those in approximation (7.28) in the general derivation of AIC in Section 7.2.

These results can be extended to any parametrized form of an exponential family model, because then we just have a 1 -to- 1 transformation from $\underline{\theta}$ to (say) $\underline{\beta}$ via some set of $K$ functions, denoted here by $\underline{W}(\underline{\theta})=\underline{\beta}$. Now $\underline{W}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=\underline{\beta}_{o}$, and let $\Sigma_{\theta}$ and $\Sigma_{\beta}$ be the variance-covariance matrices for the MLEs under the two parametrizations. An expected matrix of mixed second partial derivatives, as per (7.55) exists for the $\underline{\beta}$ parametrization; denote it by $I(\underline{\beta})$. Let the $K \times K$ Jacobian of $\underline{W}$, evaluated at $\underline{\theta}_{o}$, be

$$
J_{w}=\left\{\frac{\partial W_{i}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)}{\partial \theta_{j}}\right\}
$$

Then

$$
J_{w} \Sigma_{\theta} J_{w}^{\prime}=\Sigma_{\beta}
$$

and

$$
\left(J_{w}^{\prime}\right)^{-1} I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left(J_{w}\right)^{-1}=I\left(\underline{\beta}_{o}\right) .
$$

Both the K-L-based target and the expected log-likelihood are invariant to 1-to- 1 parameter transformations, so this must also be true for the theoretical bias correction. That is, any likelihood and MLE-based model selection criterion ought to be invariant to 1-to-1 reparametrizations of the models used. This is the case here:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{tr}\left(I\left(\underline{\beta}_{o}\right) \Sigma_{\beta}\right) & =\operatorname{tr}\left[\left(J_{w}^{\prime}\right)^{-1} I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left(J_{w}\right)^{-1} J_{w} \Sigma_{\theta} J_{w}^{\prime}\right] \\
& =\operatorname{tr}\left[\left(J_{w}^{\prime}\right)^{-1} I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \Sigma_{\theta} J_{w}^{\prime}\right] \\
& =\operatorname{tr}\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \Sigma_{\theta} J_{w}^{\prime}\left(J_{w}^{\prime}\right)^{-1}\right]=\operatorname{tr}\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \Sigma_{\theta}\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

Note, however, that if we were to estimate this trace term, the estimator might perform better under some parametrizations than under others.

One last point here: It is certainly still true that

$$
\operatorname{tr}\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) \Sigma\right]=\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]
$$

So an alternative to (7.59) is

$$
T \approx \mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}(\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}})))-\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right] .
$$

This could be directly proven here, based on the simple result

$$
\frac{\partial \log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta}))}{\partial \underline{\theta}}=\underline{S}-\mathrm{E}(\underline{S})
$$

and (7.58) to derive $\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1} J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}=\Sigma$.
For TIC we can use $\hat{J}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$ from (7.57) and from (7.55),

$$
\hat{I}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=-\frac{\partial^{2} H(\underline{\hat{\theta}})}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}},
$$

getting an estimator of $\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]$ that can be used (because of invariance) even if the parametrization of interest (and used for MLEs) is some $\underline{\beta}_{o}=\underline{W}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right), \operatorname{not} \underline{\theta}$.

Working with exponential family cases facilitates some informative evaluation of both $\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]$, relative to the value $K$, and the variability of the estimator $\operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{J}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[\hat{I}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]$. These topics, and others, are explored in the next section.

### 7.6 Evaluation of $\operatorname{tr}\left(J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right)$ and Its Estimator

The general derivation of a K-L-based model selection criterion results in (7.31) and hence (7.33). By "a general derivation," we mean a derivation in
which there is no assumption that the true data-generating distribution $f$ is one of the models $g$ in the set of models considered. Hence, the general result for K-L-based model selection does not appear to be AIC. Rather, the large-sample bias correction term subtracted from the expected maximized log-likelihood to get $T$ is $\operatorname{tr}\left(J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right)$ (Takeuchi 1976). In deriving this result there is not, and need not be, any assumption that any of the candidate models represent truth. However, in general we know with certainty that $\operatorname{tr}\left(J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right)=K$ only when $J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$ (this is sufficient but not necessary), and the latter equality is certain, in general, only when $f$ is a special case of $g$, hence, when model $g$ equals or is a generalization of "truth." This condition is unrealistic to expect, so how good is the approximation $\operatorname{tr}\left(J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right)=K$ when the truth is more general than the model, but the model is a good approximation to truth? We make here some limited, but useful, progress on this issue. Extensive theory, simulation studies, and experience (e.g., Linhart and Zucchini 1986:176-182, especially results such as in their Table 10.3) are needed to give us full confidence in when we can expect reliable results from AIC, versus when we might have to use TIC. Below, we establish theory and results for some models within the exponential family of distributions.

### 7.6.1 Comparison of AIC Versus TIC in a Very Simple Setting

We consider two simple one-parameter distributions: negative-exponential and half-normal. For each distribution we can determine the trace $\operatorname{tr}\left(J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right)$, assuming either that the distribution is truth, hence $f=g$, or that the other distribution is truth $f$ and the given distribution is a model $g$ (so $f \neq g$ ). We will also examine the estimators of the traces that can be used in TIC model selection and contrast TIC selection with AIC selection for these two distributions as models. This is a convenient situation to explore, partly because both distributions are in the exponential family.

For the negative-exponential distribution let $S=x_{1}+\cdots+x_{n}=n \bar{x}$; then

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(\underline{x} \mid \lambda) & =\frac{1}{\lambda^{n}} e^{-S / \lambda}, \\
\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \lambda)) & =-n \log (\lambda)-S / \lambda, \\
\frac{\partial}{\partial \lambda} \log (g(\underline{x} \mid \lambda)) & =-n / \lambda+S / \lambda^{2},
\end{aligned}
$$

so $\hat{\lambda}=S / n=\bar{x}$; also here $\mathrm{E}(x)=\lambda$.
Direct verification yields the following results:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& I(\lambda)=\frac{n}{\lambda^{2}} \\
& \hat{I}(\hat{\lambda})=\frac{n}{\hat{\lambda}^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

i.e., the empirical Hessian here is the same as $I(\hat{\lambda})$, and

$$
\hat{J}(\lambda)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\frac{-1}{\lambda}+\frac{x_{i}}{\lambda^{2}}\right]^{2}=\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\left(x_{i}\right)^{2}}{\lambda^{4}}\right]-\frac{n}{\lambda^{2}} .
$$

The true $J(\lambda)=\hat{\mathrm{E}}_{f}(\hat{J}(\lambda))$, and $\hat{J}(\hat{\lambda})$ is the empirical estimator of $J(\lambda)$. If we assume that the negative-exponential model is truth, then from the above (because $\mathrm{E}\left(x^{2}\right)=2 \lambda^{2}$ ),

$$
J(\lambda)=\frac{n}{\lambda^{2}}
$$

Clearly, if $f=g$, then here $\operatorname{tr}\left[J(\lambda)[I(\lambda)]^{-1}\right]=1$. The direct empirical estimator of this trace is

$$
\operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{J}(\hat{\lambda})[\hat{I}(\hat{\lambda})]^{-1}\right]=\frac{1}{n \bar{x}^{2}}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i}\right)^{2}\right]-1
$$

or

$$
\operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{J}(\hat{\lambda})[\hat{I}(\hat{\lambda})]^{-1}\right]=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i}-\bar{x}\right)^{2}}{n \bar{x}^{2}}
$$

This estimator of the trace is the same as $\frac{n-1}{n}(\widehat{\mathrm{cv}})^{2}$ (when we use $n-1$ in the denominator of the sample $s^{2}$ as per convention), which we can expect to be quite variable. The trace estimator of (7.60) does converge to 1 as $n \rightarrow \infty$ when $f=g$. This trace estimator is scale-invariant, so we can calculate its distributional properties by Monte Carlo methods with a single run of 100,000 samples at each $n$, and for each case of truth being either the negative-exponential (any value of $\lambda$ can be used) or half-normal distribution (any $\sigma$ can be used). For the case where the negative-exponential model is truth, the simulation motivated a revised, nearly unbiased, version of (7.60):

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{tr}}\left[J(\lambda)[I(\lambda)]^{-1}\right]=\frac{n}{n-1}(\widehat{\mathrm{cv}})^{2}
$$

The estimated mean and standard deviation of the estimated trace function based on (7.60) and (7.61) are given below, based on 100,000 samples:

|  | Eq. (7.60) |  | Eq. (7.61) |  |
| ---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $n$ | mean | st.dev. | mean | st.dev. |
| 20 | 0.90 | 0.37 | 1.00 | 0.41 |
| 50 | 0.96 | 0.26 | 1.00 | 0.27 |
| 100 | 0.98 | 0.19 | 1.00 | 0.20 |
| 500 | 1.00 | 0.09 | 1.00 | 0.09 |

Notice the substantial standard deviation of either trace estimator; it is this sort of large variability in the trace estimator that has given theoretical pause to routine use of TIC. We also see that the direct estimator has bias for modest sample
sizes; analytical reduction of that bias would not be practical in nontrivial applications, and yet such bias in any trace estimator might be important.

Consider now the half-normal distribution

$$
f\left(x \mid \sigma^{2}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi \sigma^{2}}} \exp \left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right)^{2}\right] .
$$

Under this distribution $\mathrm{E}\left(x^{2}\right)=\sigma^{2}$, and direct integration gives

$$
\mathrm{E}(x)=\sigma \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}
$$

If the half-normal distribution is truth and the negative-exponential is a model, then $\lambda_{o}$ as a function of $\sigma^{2}$ is found from

$$
\mathrm{E}_{f}(x)=\lambda_{o}=\sigma \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}
$$

For example, with $\sigma^{2}=1, \lambda_{o}=0.79788$ is the K-L best choice of $\lambda$.
Note the usage and concepts here: We denote the K-L best value of $\lambda$ by $\lambda_{o}$ to distinguish that the corresponding negative-exponential distribution (i.e., that based on $\lambda_{o}$ ) is the K-L best negative-exponential distribution to use as the model for the underlying truth. By denoting this value of $\lambda$ by $\lambda_{o}$ we are emphasizing that all we have is the K-L best negative-exponential model, but it may be a poor model; it certainly may not be truth.

The above expectations producing $J(\lambda)$ and $I(\lambda)$ were with respect to the negative-exponential as $g$ (ignoring what $f$ might be), but now we want to take those expectations with respect to $f$ as half-normal. Direct verification yields $I\left(\lambda_{o}\right)=n / \lambda_{o}^{2}$, whereas

$$
\begin{aligned}
J\left(\lambda_{o}\right) & =n \mathrm{E}_{f}\left[\frac{x}{\lambda_{o}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{o}}\right]^{2} \\
& =I\left(\lambda_{o}\right)\left[\frac{\sigma^{2}}{\lambda_{o}^{2}}-1\right] \\
& =I\left(\lambda_{o}\right)\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, when $f$ is half-normal and $g$ is negative-exponential,

$$
\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\lambda_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\lambda_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]=\frac{\pi}{2}-1=0.5708
$$

This trace term is not very close to 1 , the number AIC would assume. This big relative difference (i.e., 0.5708 versus 1) results because the negativeexponential model is a very poor approximation to the half-normal distribution. Note that this trace term is $<1$ ( $K$ is 1 here).



Next we determine the trace estimator under the half-normal as the model. We therein have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma}^{2} & =\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i}\right)^{2}}{n}, \\
I\left(\sigma^{2}\right) & =\frac{n}{2 \sigma^{4}}, \\
\hat{J}\left(\sigma^{2}\right) & =\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\frac{-1}{2 \sigma^{2}}+\frac{\left(x_{i}\right)^{2}}{2 \sigma^{4}}\right]^{2} \\
& =I\left(\sigma^{2}\right)\left[\frac{1}{2 n}\right] \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\frac{\left(x_{i}\right)^{2}}{\sigma^{2}}-1\right]^{2} .
\end{aligned}
$$

The key part of the empirical estimator $\hat{J}\left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)$ is again a squared coefficient of variation, but here it is for the variable $x^{2}$. Denote this $\widehat{\mathrm{cv}}$ by $\widehat{\mathrm{cv}}\left(x^{2}\right)$, and we can use the notation

$$
\hat{J}\left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)=\hat{I}\left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\left[\frac{n-1}{2 n}\right]\left[\widehat{\operatorname{cv}}\left(x^{2}\right)\right]^{2}
$$

Thus, for the half-normal distribution being the model, the TIC estimator of the trace is

$$
\operatorname{tr}\left[\hat{J}\left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\left[\hat{I}\left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]=\left[\frac{n-1}{2 n}\right]\left[\widehat{\operatorname{cv}}\left(x^{2}\right)\right]^{2}
$$

If truth is the half-normal model, this quantity will converge to 1 ; and again, (7.62) is scale-invariant. For the half-normal model as truth we computed the mean and standard deviation of (7.62) by Monte Carlo methods with 100,000 samples at each $n$. This led to a nearly unbiased version of (7.62),

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{tr}}\left[J\left(\sigma^{2}\right)\left[I\left(\sigma^{2}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{n}{n-1}\right]^{2}\left[\widehat{\mathrm{cv}}\left(x^{2}\right)\right]^{2}
$$

that we then also used in the simulations. The results are below:

|  | Eq. (7.62) |  | Eq. (7.63) |  |
| ---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $n$ | mean | st.dev. | mean | st.dev. |
| 20 | 0.87 | 0.39 | 1.01 | 0.45 |
| 50 | 0.94 | 0.30 | 1.00 | 0.32 |
| 100 | 0.97 | 0.23 | 1.00 | 0.23 |
| 500 | 0.99 | 0.11 | 1.00 | 0.11 |

The main point from the above is how variable the trace estimator is.
To complete a set of analytical results (useful for validating Monte Carlo results) we computed the value of $\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\sigma_{o}^{2}\right)\left[I\left(\sigma_{o}^{2}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]$ for the half-normal model when truth is the negative-exponential distribution. First we need $\sigma_{o}^{2}=\mathrm{E}\left(x^{2}\right)=2 \lambda^{2}$, because the expectation of $x$ must be taken with respect to the negative-exponential distribution. We find that for the half-normal
model and the negative-exponential as truth,

$$
J\left(\sigma_{o}^{2}\right)=I\left(\sigma_{o}^{2}\right)\left[\frac{1}{2}\right] \mathrm{E}\left[\frac{x^{2}}{\sigma_{o}^{2}}-1\right]^{2}=2.5 I\left(\sigma_{o}^{2}\right)
$$

Thus when truth is the negative-exponential, and the model is half-normal (a terrible model in this case), $\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\sigma_{o}^{2}\right)\left[I\left(\sigma_{o}^{2}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]=2.5$ (not 1 as AIC assumes). Done the other way around we had the trace $<1$. It turns out that this trace function under model misspecification can be either above or below $K$; it varies by situation, and in some situations the trace function can equal $K$ even with a misspecified model.

We can now compare AIC versus TIC model selection when the choices and truth are negative-exponential or half-normal.
For the negative-exponential model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{AIC} & =2 n[\log (\bar{x})+1]+2 \\
\mathrm{TIC} & =2 n[\log (\bar{x})+1]+2 \frac{n-1}{n}(\widehat{\mathrm{cv}}(x))^{2} \\
\mathrm{TICu} & =2 n[\log (\bar{x})+1]+2 \frac{n}{n-1}(\widehat{\mathrm{cv}}(x))^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the half-normal model (using $\hat{\sigma}^{2}=$ mean of the $x_{i}^{2}$ ):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{AIC} & =n\left[\log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)+1-\log (2 / \pi)\right]+2 \\
\mathrm{TIC} & =n\left[\log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)+1-\log (2 / \pi)\right]+\left[\frac{n-1}{n}\right]^{2}\left[\widehat{\mathrm{cv}}\left(x^{2}\right)\right]^{2} \\
\mathrm{TICu} & =n\left[\log \left(\hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)+1-\log (2 / \pi)\right]+\left[\frac{n}{n-1}\right]^{2}\left[\widehat{\mathrm{cv}}\left(x^{2}\right)\right]^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

In both cases here TICu means just that the estimator of $\left.\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\theta_{o}\right)\right)\left[I\left(\theta_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]$ is almost unbiased, as opposed to the direct, biased, plug-in estimators of the needed coefficients of variation.

Table 7.1 shows some results. The point of this brief comparison was to learn something about AIC versus TIC in a simple setting, especially whether or not they would give greatly different results. The context here is so simple that only two models are compared. Moreover, one or the other model was used as the data-generating distribution (i.e., truth). We did not consider prediction here, so the only possible criterion to use to compare performance of AIC versus TIC is rate of selection of the true model. We did not wish to do, in this book, any serious evaluation of AIC versus TIC under full-blown realistic conditions of complex truth, and a set of approximating models, wherein the correct basis of evaluation is how well a selection procedure does at selecting the K-L best model (technically, we would be selecting the expected K-L best model).

Several inferences supported by Table 7.1, and by all other sample sizes examined for this situation, surprised us. For the case that the negativeexponential model is true, the selection results based on TIC were uniformly

TABLE 7.1. Percentage of correct selection when one of the models (negative-exponential or half-normal) is truth, based on 100,000 samples; see text for AIC, TIC, and TICu formulas; average percent correct is based on equal weighting of the two cases.
| sample size, $n$ | selection criterion | truth |  | average percent correct |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
|  |  | negative expon. | halfnormal |  |
| 20 | AIC | 64 | 85 | 75 |
|  | TIC | 73 | 77 | 75 |
|  | TICu | 75 | 75 | 75 |
| 50 | AIC | 82 | 92 | 87 |
|  | TIC | 87 | 87 | 87 |
|  | TICu | 87 | 87 | 87 |
| 100 | AIC | 93 | 97 | 95 |
|  | TIC | 95 | 95 | 95 |
|  | TICu | 95 | 95 | 95 |
| 500 | AIC | 100 | 100 | 100 |
|  | TIC | 100 | 100 | 100 |
|  | TICu | 100 | 100 | 100 |


as good or better than those under AIC. The improvement is not large except at small sample sizes, wherein an "AIC" should be used anyway. Conversely, for the case that the half-normal model is true, the selection results based on AIC were uniformly as good or better than those under TIC. In either case, bias-correction of the trace estimator makes no real difference. We would not know a priori which (if either) model was true. If we compute an average percent-correct selection based on the idea that we have no information to justify any weighting other than a 50:50 weighting of these results, we get, on average, no advantage at all for TIC over AIC. Clearly, we do not know the extent to which these results would generalize.

### 7.6.2 Evaluation Under Logistic Regression

Logistic regression is used often, therefore we illustrate that it is a case of an exponential family model, and we explore the above trace question for this model. Let $x_{i}$ be a Bernoulli random variable with true probability $\mu_{i}$ of being 1 (and probability $1-\mu_{i}$ of being 0 ). For a sample of $n$ independent $x_{i}$ we base analysis on some assumed model for the $\mu_{i}$. In order to distinguish truth from model we adopt the notation for the model as $p_{i} \equiv p_{i}(\underline{\theta})$, for some structure imposed on these $p_{i}$, as a function of a $K$-dimensional parameter vector $\underline{\theta}$. The relevant pdf, or likelihood (the same notation continues to serve this dual
role), for the model is

$$
g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta})=\prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(p_{i}\right)^{x_{i}}\left(q_{i}\right)^{1-x_{i}}
$$

We assume that known covariates $\underline{z}_{i}$, as $K \times 1$ column vectors, are associated with each observation, $x_{i}$, and an explanatory structural model is

$$
p_{i}=\frac{1}{1+e^{-\underline{z_{i}^{\prime}} \underline{\theta}}}, \text { or } q_{i}=\frac{e^{-z_{i}^{\prime} \underline{\theta}}}{1+e^{-z_{i}^{\prime} \underline{\theta}}},
$$

which is equivalent to

$$
\log \left[p_{i} /\left(1-p_{i}\right)\right]=\underline{z}_{i}^{\prime} \underline{\theta} .
$$

A modest amount of algebra gives the result

$$
g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\theta})=\exp \left[\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i} \underline{z}_{i}\right)\right]^{\prime} \underline{\theta}+\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(-\log \left(1+e^{\underline{z}_{i}^{\prime}} \underline{\theta}\right)\right)\right]\right],
$$

which is in the canonical form of the exponential family for

$$
H(\underline{\theta})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(-\log \left(1+e^{z_{i}^{\prime} \underline{\theta}}\right)\right)
$$

and

$$
\underline{S}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i} \underline{z}_{i}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \underline{s}_{i}
$$

$(G(\underline{\theta})=0)$. We will need the true expectation of $\underline{S}$ :

$$
\mathrm{E}_{f}(\underline{S})=\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\mu_{i} \underline{z}_{i}\right)\right] .
$$

Also, from (7.64), $H(\underline{\theta})=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \left(1-p_{i}\right)$ is an equivalent form for $H(\underline{\theta})$.
Two key quantities we need are

$$
I(\underline{\theta})=-\frac{\partial^{2} H(\underline{\theta})}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}}
$$

(see 7.55) and $-\partial H(\underline{\theta}) / \partial \underline{\theta}=\mathrm{E}_{f}(\underline{S})$. Some straightforward mathematics leads to the results

$$
-\frac{\partial H(\underline{\theta})}{\partial \underline{\theta}}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_{i} \underline{z}_{i}
$$

and

$$
I(\underline{\theta})=-\frac{\partial^{2} H(\underline{\theta})}{\partial \underline{\theta}^{2}}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_{i} q_{i} \underline{z}_{i} \underline{z}_{i}^{\prime} .
$$

These formulas can be put in matrix notation. To do so we define an $n \times 1$ column vector $\underline{P}(\underline{\theta})$ with $i$ th element $p_{i}(\underline{\theta})$, and an $n \times n$ diagonal matrix $V_{p}$ with $i$ th diagonal element $p_{i} q_{i}$, and an $n \times K$ matrix $Z$ where the $i$ th row is $z_{i}^{\prime}$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{E}_{f}(\underline{S}) & =Z^{\prime} \underline{\mu}, \\
-\frac{\partial H(\underline{\theta})}{\partial \underline{\theta}} & =Z^{\prime} \underline{P}(\underline{\theta}),
\end{aligned}
$$

and

$$
I(\underline{\theta})=Z^{\prime} V_{p} Z .
$$

The MLE $\underline{\hat{\theta}}$ is found by setting (7.65) to $\underline{S}$ and solving the resultant $K$ nonlinear equations for $\underline{\theta}$, hence solving $\underline{S}=Z^{\prime} \underline{P}(\underline{\hat{\theta}})$. The true parameter value $\underline{\theta}_{o}$ that applies here, given truth $\underline{\mu}$ and the model, is found by solving the same $K$ equations but with $\underline{S}$ replaced by its true expectation, hence solving

$$
Z^{\prime} \underline{\mu}=Z^{\prime} \underline{P}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right),
$$

or

$$
Z^{\prime}\left(\underline{\mu}-\underline{P}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right)=\underline{0} .
$$

In partly nonmatrix notation, we solve

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\mu_{i}-p_{i}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right) \underline{z}_{i}=\underline{0} .
$$

If truth $\underline{\mu}$ is not given exactly by the assumed model evaluated at $\underline{\theta}_{o}$, then $\underline{\mu}=\underline{P}\left(\overline{\underline{\theta}_{o}}\right)$ will not hold even though the above equations will have a unique solution in $\underline{\theta}_{o}$, just as the MLE equations will have a unique solution as $\underline{\hat{\theta}}$.

To proceed we also need to know the general formula for $J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$. From (7.12) we have

$$
J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=\mathrm{E}_{f}\left[\left[\underline{S}-Z^{\prime} \underline{P}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]\left[\underline{S}-Z^{\prime} \underline{P}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{\prime}\right]
$$

In partly nonmatrix form this formula is

$$
\begin{aligned}
J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right) & =\mathrm{E}_{f}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\underline{s}_{i}-p_{i} \underline{z}_{i}\right)\right]\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\underline{s}_{i}-p_{i} \underline{z}_{i}\right)\right]^{\prime} \\
& =\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathrm{E}_{f}\left(\underline{s}_{i}-p_{i} \underline{z}_{i}\right)\left(\underline{s}_{j}-p_{j} \underline{z}_{j}\right)^{\prime}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, using $\underline{s}_{i}=x_{i} \underline{z}_{i}$ and $\mathrm{E}_{f}\left(x_{i}\right)=\mu_{i}$ the above becomes

$$
J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathrm{E}_{f}\left(x_{i}-p_{i}\right)\left(x_{j}-p_{j}\right)\left[\underline{z}_{i} \underline{z}_{j}^{\prime}\right]
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
= & \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{E}_{f}\left(x_{i}-p_{i}\right)^{2}\left[\underline{z}_{i} \underline{z}_{i}^{\prime}\right]+\sum^{n} \sum_{i \neq j}^{n} \mathrm{E}_{f}\left(x_{i}-p_{i}\right)\left(x_{j}-p_{j}\right)\left[\underline{z}_{i} \underline{z}_{j}^{\prime}\right] \\
= & \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\left(\mu_{i}\left(1-\mu_{i}\right)\right)+\left(\mu_{i}-p_{i}\right)^{2}\right]\left[\underline{z}_{i} \underline{z}_{i}^{\prime}\right] \\
& +\sum^{n} \sum_{i \neq j}^{n}\left(\mu_{i}-p_{i}\right)\left(\mu_{j}-p_{j}\right)\left[\underline{z}_{i} \underline{z}_{j}^{\prime}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Completing the square in the trailing term above, we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)= & \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\left(\mu_{i}\left(1-\mu_{i}\right)\right)+\left(\mu_{i}-p_{i}\right)^{2}\right]\left[\underline{z}_{i} \underline{z}_{i}^{\prime}\right] \\
& +\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\mu_{i}-p_{i}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right) \underline{z}_{i}\right]\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\mu_{i}-p_{i}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right) \underline{z}_{i}\right]^{\prime} \\
& -\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\left(\mu_{i}-p_{i}\right)^{2}\right]\left[\underline{z}_{i} \underline{z}_{i}^{\prime}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The middle term of the above is zero because of the equation defining $\underline{\theta}_{o}$, and the third term cancels with part of the first term, so we have

$$
J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_{i}\left(1-\mu_{i}\right)\left[\underline{z}_{i} \underline{z}_{i}^{\prime}\right]
$$

or in pure matrix terms,

$$
J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=Z^{\prime} V_{\mu} Z
$$

Here, $V_{\mu}$ is an $n \times n$ diagonal matrix with $i$ th diagonal element $\mu_{i}\left(1-\mu_{i}\right)$. Contrast (7.67) to $I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)=Z^{\prime} V_{p} Z$.

It is easy, but not very informative, now to write

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right] & =\operatorname{tr}\left[\left(Z^{\prime} V_{\mu} Z\right)\left(Z^{\prime} V_{p} Z\right)^{-1}\right] \\
& =K+\operatorname{tr}\left[\left(Z^{\prime}\left(V_{\mu}-V_{p}\right) Z\right)\left(Z^{\prime} V_{p} Z\right)^{-1}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The above makes it easier to realize that the trace term is exactly $K$ if for all $i \mu_{i}\left(1-\mu_{i}\right)=p_{i}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left(1-p_{i}\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right)$. However, these equalities may fail to hold, yet we can still get $\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]=K$; hence this latter equality can hold with a model that does not match truth, i.e., where $g \subset f$ with strict inequality.

The above results are totally general, so they apply to the case where, say, $w$ replicate observations are taken at each of $j=1, \ldots, r$ covariate values. The total sample size is then $n=r * w$, but we will have only $r$ different values of $\mu_{j}$ to specify for truth and only $r$ values of $p_{j}(\underline{\theta})$ to consider under any model. Hence, to gain some insights here we used the simple model $\log \left[p_{j} /\left(1-p_{j}\right)\right]=a+b j$ for $j=1, \ldots, r$, with $w$ replicate observations at each $j$. Thus $K=2, \underline{\theta}=(a, b)^{\prime}$, and $\underline{z}_{j}=(1, j)^{\prime}$. In fact, for numerical
or analytical results we do not have to specify what $w$ is (just $r$ is needed), and we can actually proceed as if $n=r$ with just one sample at each value of $j$. However, the results so derived apply reasonably well only to cases where $n=r * w$ would be "large," say 100 or more (given $K=2$ ). Thus to explore the trace term under this use of a simple logistic regression model we need only specify a set of $\mu_{1}, \ldots, \mu_{r}$, solve

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{r}\left(\mu_{i}-\left(a_{o}+b_{o} i\right)\right) \underline{z}_{i}=\underline{0}
$$

for $\underline{\theta}_{o}=\left(a_{o}, b_{o}\right)^{\prime}$, and compute $J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$ (7.67), $I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$ (7.66), and then $\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]$ (also denoted by "bias"). In doing this we focused on sets of $\mu$ that were near to fitting the logistic structural model, either by generating a $\underline{p}(\underline{\theta})$ vector that fit the model, then perturbing some (or all) of the $p_{j}$, or by starting with $\mu_{j}=j /(r+1)$, which is not a logistic regression structural model but is not too far from fitting such a structural model.

With at most modest deviation of truth from any actual simple logistic regression model structure we found that the trace term value stayed near $K=2$ (between about 1.8 and 2 , sometimes going a little above 2 , say to 2.1 ). For the case of truth being the simple linear model ( $\mu_{j}=j /(r+1)$ ), the trace term varied monotonically from 1.98 at $r=5$, to 1.91 at $r=50$. Table 7.2 gives some results for $r=10$, based on truth being perturbed values from the logistic model logit $\left(p_{j}\right)=3.0-0.5 j$. The first line of Table 7.2 gives the true $\mu_{j}$ computed from this model (scaled by 1,000 ).

What one can see in Table 7.2 (and other computations we did corroborate this) is that the true $\mu_{j}$ have to be here a very poor approximation to an exact simple logistic model before the trace term deviates much from $K=2$. Thus, if the data seem at all well fit by a logistic model, then the use of trace $=K$ (as opposed to any attempted estimation of the trace) seems quite suitable. This is especially important here because $V_{\mu}$, hence $J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)$, cannot be estimated at all unless there is replication at each $\underline{z}_{i}$, and there would need to be substantial such replication; this condition rarely occurs with logistic regression.

Formula (7.68) was corroborated by direct Monte Carlo evaluation of the target bias (trace term) for a few cases in Table 7.2. The completely general, and hence most direct, way to do this is to evaluate using simulation the value of

$$
\operatorname{bias}=\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})))]-\mathrm{E}_{\underline{x}}[\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x})))] .
$$

Hence for one Monte Carlo replicate (generating iid $\underline{x}$ and $\underline{y}$ ) we get

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{bias}}=\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{y})))-\log (g(\underline{x} \mid \underline{\hat{\theta}}(\underline{x}))) .
$$

Averaged over many samples ( $m$ ), if large sample size $n$ is used, the average $\widehat{\text { bias will equal } \operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right] \text {. }}$

For many models the first term on the right-hand side of (7.69) will be linear in $\underline{x}$, so we can analytically take the expectation with respect to $\underline{x}$. For this

TABLE 7.2. Some values of trace $=\operatorname{tr}\left[J\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\left[I\left(\underline{\theta}_{o}\right)\right]^{-1}\right]$ for the simple logistic model $\operatorname{logit}\left(p_{j}\right)=a+b j,(j=1, \ldots, 10)$ fit to $\mu_{j}$ as perturbed values of $p_{j}$ from $\operatorname{logit}\left(p_{j}\right)= 3-0.5 j$; values of $\mu_{j}$ are shown, scaled by 1,000 ; case one (i.e., the first line) exactly fits the logistic model, but none of the other cases are a perfect fit to the assumed model form. The results are reasonably applicable if $w$ is at least 10 or 20 .
| $\mu_{1}$ | $\mu_{2}$ | $\mu_{3}$ | $\mu_{4}$ | $\mu_{5}$ | $\mu_{6}$ | $\mu_{7}$ | $\mu_{8}$ | $\mu_{9}$ | $\mu_{10}$ | trace |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 924 | 881 | 818 | 731 | 622 | 500 | 378 | 269 | 182 | 119 | 2 |
| 900 | 900 | 900 | 900 | 622 | 500 | 378 | 269 | 182 | 119 | 2.063 |
| 924 | 881 | 970 | 757 | 725 | 530 | 410 | 231 | 186 | 134 | 2.047 |
| 900 | 944 | 898 | 773 | 666 | 522 | 349 | 264 | 176 | 127 | 2.046 |
| 864 | 993 | 971 | 821 | 564 | 514 | 366 | 236 | 150 | 135 | 2.023 |
| 894 | 990 | 870 | 826 | 583 | 457 | 372 | 252 | 216 | 127 | 2.00011 |
| 924 | 720 | 818 | 796 | 749 | 632 | 492 | 188 | 260 | 164 | 1.993 |
| 924 | 881 | 768 | 831 | 622 | 400 | 378 | 269 | 282 | 119 | 1.980 |
| 924 | 952 | 650 | 838 | 638 | 448 | 278 | 189 | 204 | 132 | 1.967 |
| 874 | 874 | 874 | 558 | 558 | 558 | 558 | 190 | 190 | 190 | 1.925 |
| 924 | 881 | 818 | 731 | 400 | 600 | 378 | 269 | 182 | 119 | 1.925 |
| 924 | 881 | 818 | 731 | 300 | 700 | 378 | 269 | 182 | 119 | 1.857 |
| 924 | 881 | 818 | 731 | 622 | 500 | 378 | 269 | 182 | 119 | 1.828 |
| 924 | 881 | 568 | 831 | 622 | 100 | 378 | 269 | 432 | 119 | 1.774 |
| 924 | 881 | 818 | 731 | 200 | 800 | 378 | 269 | 182 | 119 | 1.768 |
| 674 | 981 | 818 | 731 | 622 | 500 | 128 | 669 | 182 | 119 | 1.765 |
| 924 | 881 | 818 | 731 | 622 | 500 | 900 | 900 | 900 | 900 | 1.494 |


logistic example we thus get, expressed in basic form,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\widehat{\operatorname{bias}}= & \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[n_{i} \mu_{i} \log \left(p_{i}(\underline{\hat{\theta}})\right)+n_{i}\left(1-\mu_{i}\right) \log \left(q_{i}(\underline{\hat{\theta}})\right)\right] \\
& -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{E}_{\underline{y}}\left[y_{i} \log \left(p_{i}(\underline{\hat{\theta}})\right)+\left(n_{i}-y_{i}\right) \log \left(q_{i}(\underline{\hat{\theta}})\right)\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

Here the MLE $\underline{\hat{\theta}}$ is based on data $\underline{y}$.
For direct Monte Carlo evaluation we used replicate covariate values, as noted above with the same number of replicates $w$ for each $j=1, \ldots, r$. Then we generated a large number $m$ of independent samples from the generating model, fit the model-based MLE to each sample, computed $\widehat{\text { bias }}$ using (7.70), by sample, and got its average and empirical standard error.

All this is quite obvious; where we are going here is that this direct Monte Carlo evaluation is poor in the sense of needing a huge number of samples. The problem is that as $w$ (i.e., $n$ ) increases, the number of samples needed to get a small standard error (like 0.005) on the estimate bias increases because the variance of $\widehat{\text { bias }}$, for one sample, increases with increasing sample size, and that variance can be quite large. For a large sample size $n$ (which is required for
the trace approximation to hold very well) it can take one million Monte Carlo samples to get even a moderately small standard error on estimated bias. For example, for the case in Table 7.2 where the trace is computed to be 1.774 , for $w=100$ (hence sample size $n=1,000$ Bernoulli trials) for one representative set of 10,000 $(=m)$ Monte Carlo samples we got average $\widehat{\text { bias }}=1.528$ with an estimated standard error of 0.135 . Other runs verified that it takes about one million Monte Carlo samples to get a standard error (on estimated bias) of about 0.014 with $w=100$ in this example. But we might need a bigger $w$ for the trace formula to apply exactly; for $w=1,000$ and 10,000 Monte Carlo samples we got the average $\widehat{\text { bias }}=1.982$ with $\widehat{\mathrm{se}}=0.424$. This phenomenon is the reverse of what we expect; i.e., we expect to get increasing precision (for the same number, $m$, of Monte Carlo samples) as sample size $n$ increases. The reverse phenomenon occurs here because the expected difference in likelihoods involved in direct computation of bias (i.e., (7.70)) is constant independent of sample size $n$, but the variance of each of those two likelihood sums in (7.70) is proportional to $n$ and the two terms are not highly correlated. Thus as sample size $n$ increases, the precision of the estimated bias, given a fixed number of Monte Carlo samples ( $m$ ), actually decreases. So to evaluate well, with this brute-force approach, the adequacy of the trace term approximation at large sample sizes, it takes a huge number of Monte Carlo samples.

With models that are in the exponential family there is an alternative way to do exact Monte Carlo evaluation of the bias that must be subtracted from the maximized log-likelihood for exact K-L based model selection. Formula (7.54) is an exact result for any sample size:

$$
\text { bias }=\operatorname{tr}[\operatorname{COV}(\underline{\hat{\theta}}, \underline{S})] .
$$

While $\underline{\theta}$ and $\underline{S}$ are only for the canonical form of the model, the result will apply for any parametrization of the assumed model because of the invariance of the result to 1 -to- 1 transformations of $\underline{\theta}$ (see end of Section 7.5). Thus the alternative Monte Carlo evaluation is simply to take for each sample the already computed MLE and minimal sufficient statistic and, from this set of records of size $m$, estimate the covariances $\operatorname{cov}\left(\hat{\theta}_{i}, S_{i}\right), i=1, \ldots, K$, and then sum these $K$ estimates. The result is $\widehat{\text { bias }}$, and this approach is much more efficient. For the same case in Table 7.2 (i.e., trace $=1.774$ ), using $w=100(r=10$, hence $n=1,000$ ) and 10,000 Monte Carlo samples we got $\widehat{\text { bias }}=1.804$ and its $\widehat{\text { se }}=0.019$ using the covariance approach. Based on this and other runs there was a clear suggestion that $w=100$ was not quite big enough for the trace (7.68) to apply reliably to three digits (it was then reliable to two digits). Using $w=1,000$ and 10,000 Monte Carlo samples we got $\widehat{\text { bias }}=1.771$, $\widehat{\mathrm{se}}=0.017$.
